title: Beacon proxies can be left uninitialized and then fully hijacked
claim: `UpgradeBeaconProxy` explicitly allows deployment with empty initialization calldata. `Replica.initialize` is a public `initializer` that sets ownership, updater, remote domain, committed root, and optimistic timeout via `__NomadBase_initialize`. If a beacon proxy is ever deployed without constructor-time init data, the first external caller can initialize it through the proxy and seize full control of the replica.
impact: A forgotten or failed initialization becomes a full bridge takeover. The attacker can become owner, choose an attacker-controlled updater and trusted root, then prove and process forged messages, enabling arbitrary cross-chain message execution and asset theft.
paths: Before the intended operator initializes it, an attacker calls `Replica.initialize(...)` through the proxy.
The initializer makes the attacker the owner and sets attacker-chosen updater/root parameters.
The attacker proves messages against the attacker-chosen committed root and processes arbitrary payloads on the destination chain.

title: Updater signatures are replayable across deployments that reuse the same domain and updater key
claim: The updater signature digest is only `keccak256(homeDomainHash(), oldRoot, newRoot)`, and `homeDomainHash()` only binds the 32-bit remote domain plus the string `NOMAD`. It does not bind the signature to a specific Home contract, Replica contract, chain id, or deployment instance.
impact: If two deployments ever reuse the same remote domain and updater key, a valid update signed for one deployment is also valid on the other. That can import foreign roots into the wrong replica and authorize processing of messages that never belonged to that bridge, leading to cross-environment fund theft or arbitrary message execution.
paths: A second deployment is configured with the same `remoteDomain` and `updater` as an existing deployment.
The updater signs `(oldRoot, newRoot)` for deployment A.
Anyone relays the same signature to deployment B while B has the same current `committedRoot`.
Deployment B accepts deployment A's root and starts proving/processing messages from the wrong environment.

title: Replica silently truncates 32-byte recipients to 20 bytes and dispatches to non-contract addresses
claim: The message format commits to a full 32-byte recipient, but execution converts it with `address(uint160(uint256(_buf)))`, silently discarding the upper 12 bytes. `process()` then calls `handle` on the truncated address without verifying that code exists there or that the target actually implements the expected interface.
impact: Malformed or non-canonical recipient encodings can be misdelivered or black-holed on the destination chain. A message can be marked processed even when it was routed to an EOA or unintended contract, causing irreversible loss of the bridged action and potential permanent lockup of user funds.
paths: A message is committed with a 32-byte recipient whose upper 12 bytes are non-zero or whose lower 20 bytes resolve to an EOA/wrong contract.
`recipientAddress()` truncates the recipient to the low 20 bytes.
`process()` dispatches to that truncated address without any code/interface check.
The message is irrevocably marked processed even though the intended recipient logic never ran.
